steps.prompt: Which word is the main verb?

hints.Nulla: 'Nulla' is an adjective meaning 'no / not any'
hints.monstro: 'monstro' is a noun in the ablative — 'from that monster'

hints.to be compared: 'comparare' can mean 'to compare' in some contexts, but here it means 'to prepare/contrive'
hints.to be bought: 'to be bought' is 'emi' — 'comparari' here means 'to be prepared/contrived'
hints.to be gathered: 'to be gathered' is 'colligi' — 'comparari' means 'to be prepared against'

steps.prompt: What tense, mood, and voice is 'comparabitur'?

hints.Present passive indicative: Present passive would be 'comparatur' — 'comparabitur' with -bitur is future
hints.Future active indicative: Future active would be 'comparabit' — 'comparabitur' with passive -bitur ending is passive
hints.Present passive subjunctive: Present passive subjunctive would be 'comparetur' — 'comparabitur' is future indicative

hints.paro, parare, paravi, paratum: These are the parts of 'parare' (to prepare) — the base verb without com- prefix
hints.compello, compellere, compuli, compulsum: These are the parts of 'compellere' (to compel)
hints.compono, componere, composui, compositum: These are the parts of 'componere' (to arrange/compose)

hints.Genitive: Genitive would be 'perniciei' — 'pernicies' is nominative, subject of 'comparabitur'

hints.1st: 1st declension nouns end in -a — 'pernicies, perniciei' is 5th declension
hints.3rd: 3rd declension nominatives vary, but 'pernicies, perniciei' with gen. -ei is 5th declension
hints.4th: 4th declension nouns have gen. -us — 'pernicies, perniciei' has gen. -ei (5th)

hints.Nominative: Nominative would be 'monstrum' — 'monstro' with 'a' is ablative of agent
hints.Dative: Dative would be 'monstro' (same form), but 'a monstro' with the preposition signals ablative of agent

steps.prompt: What does calling Catiline 'monstro illo' achieve rhetorically?
steps.target: Dehumanization — Catiline is no longer a man but a monster
choices: Dehumanization — Catiline is no longer a man but a monster
A reference to a religious omen

hints.A compliment to Catiline's power: 'monstrum' means a monstrous creature — it is an insult, not a compliment
hints.A reference to a religious omen: While 'monstrum' can mean 'portent,' here it dehumanizes Catiline as a moral monster
hints.A neutral description: 'monstrum' is strongly pejorative — Cicero strips Catiline of his humanity

translation: No destruction will any longer be contrived by that monster.
